question: What is meant by 'ventilation'?
answer: The movement of air into and out of the lungs
accept: air moving in and out of the lungs
breathing air in and out
misconceptions: it is only breathing in
it is gas exchange in the alveoli
it is the same as inhaling

question: A pupil says ventilation and inhaling mean the same thing. Why is this wrong?
answer: Ventilation is air moving both in and out, but inhaling is only the breathing-in part
options: Ventilation is air moving both in and out, but inhaling is only the breathing-in part
Ventilation is breathing out, while inhaling is breathing in
Ventilation only happens during exercise, while inhaling happens at rest
Ventilation is gas exchange in the blood, while inhaling is in the lungs
distractors: ventilation covers both directions, not just exhaling
ventilation happens all the time, not only during exercise
ventilation is the movement of air, not gas exchange in the blood
misconceptions: inhaling and ventilation are the same thing
ventilation means breathing out only

question: Name, in order, the four structures air passes through after the nose or mouth on its way to the air sacs.
answer: trachea
bronchi
bronchioles
alveoli
accept: windpipe (for trachea)
bronchus (for bronchi)
misconceptions: bronchioles before bronchi
air goes straight from trachea to alveoli
oesophagus instead of trachea

question: Explain why the trachea has rings of cartilage.
answer: The cartilage rings hold the trachea open
so gases can pass through easily / so it does not collapse under the pressure in the chest
accept: keeps the windpipe open
stops it collapsing when we breathe
misconceptions: the cartilage traps dust
the cartilage produces mucus
the rings help air diffuse faster

question: Describe what the intercostal muscles and the diaphragm do when you breathe in.
answer: The intercostal muscles contract (moving the ribcage up and out)
the diaphragm flattens and moves down
accept: ribs move up and out
diaphragm contracts / flattens
misconceptions: the diaphragm moves up when breathing in
the intercostal muscles relax when breathing in
the diaphragm domes upwards to fill the lungs

question: Explain why air rushes into the lungs when you breathe in.
answer: The intercostal muscles contract and the diaphragm flattens
this increases the volume of the chest
so the pressure inside the lungs drops/falls, and air rushes in
accept: chest gets bigger so pressure falls
lower pressure inside lungs draws air in
misconceptions: the lungs suck air in like a vacuum on their own
the lungs expand first and pull the chest open
pressure increases so air is pushed in

question: Explain what happens to the chest volume and lung pressure when you breathe out, and why this forces air out.
answer: The intercostal muscles relax and the diaphragm moves up, decreasing the volume of the chest
this increases the pressure inside the lungs
so air is forced out
accept: chest gets smaller so pressure rises
higher pressure pushes air out
misconceptions: the diaphragm flattens when breathing out
volume increases when breathing out
pressure drops so air leaves

question: In the bell jar model of the lungs, what do the balloons and the rubber sheet each represent?
answer: The balloons represent the lungs
the rubber sheet represents the diaphragm
accept: balloons = lungs
rubber sheet = diaphragm
misconceptions: the rubber sheet represents the ribs
the balloons represent the alveoli
the bell jar represents the diaphragm

question: Give two ways in which the bell jar model differs from a real thorax.
answer: The bell jar (ribcage) does not have intercostal muscles and does not move during breathing
the rubber sheet has to be pulled down, but in reality the diaphragm flattens / the balloons do not show oxygen and carbon dioxide moving in and out
accept: the ribs do not move in the model
the Y-tube does not show the cartilage/flexibility of the trachea
misconceptions: the model is identical to a real thorax
the balloons show gas exchange happening
the bell jar moves like real ribs

question: Which sheet of muscle sits underneath the lungs and helps with breathing?
answer: The diaphragm
distractors: the intercostal muscles sit between the ribs, not under the lungs
the bronchioles are airway tubes, not a muscle
cartilage rings hold the trachea open and are not a muscle
misconceptions: the intercostal muscles are under the lungs
the diaphragm is above the lungs

question: A pupil takes 5 breaths in 20 seconds. Use breaths in 20 seconds x 3 to work out their breathing rate in breaths per minute.
answer: 15 breaths per minute
working: breaths per minute = breaths in 20 s x 3 = 5 x 3 = 15
misconceptions: multiplying by 20 instead of 3
leaving the answer as 5
dividing by 3

question: What is the function of the cilia and the mucus that line the trachea?
answer: The mucus traps dust and germs
the cilia sweep the mucus up and out of the trachea
accept: cilia move mucus out of the windpipe
mucus catches dirt and microbes
misconceptions: cilia sweep germs out directly (must be via mucus)
mucus is produced by the cilia
cartilage traps the dust

question: Explain why your breathing rate increases when you exercise.
answer: So that more oxygen can enter the lungs and diffuse into the blood
and more carbon dioxide (produced by the cells) can be removed
accept: to take in more oxygen
to get rid of extra carbon dioxide
misconceptions: to take in more carbon dioxide
to cool the body down
because the lungs get bigger permanently

question: Which gas builds up in the cells during exercise and must be removed by faster breathing?
answer: Carbon dioxide
distractors: oxygen is used up during exercise, it does not build up
nitrogen is breathed in and out unchanged and is not produced by cells
water vapour is exhaled but is not the waste gas that builds up from exercise
misconceptions: oxygen builds up during exercise
nitrogen is the waste gas produced by cells

question: Aysha works at a desk all day while Nadia is an athlete who is always running. Explain why Nadia has a higher breathing rate than Aysha.
answer: Nadia exercises so she needs a lot more oxygen
and needs to remove a lot more carbon dioxide, so her breathing rate is higher
accept: she uses more oxygen when running
she produces more carbon dioxide that must be removed
misconceptions: Nadia has bigger lungs so always breathes faster
Aysha needs more oxygen because she sits still
breathing rate does not depend on activity

question: What is asthma?
answer: A condition where the lining of the airways becomes irritated and inflamed, making it harder to breathe
accept: airways become inflamed and swollen
a condition that inflames the airways
misconceptions: a condition caused by the lungs filling with mucus only
an infection of the lungs
a disease only caused by smoking

question: Describe three things that happen to the airways during an asthma attack that make breathing harder.
answer: The airways (trachea, bronchi and bronchioles) become inflamed/swollen
the muscles surrounding the airways contract
the goblet cells release too much mucus, so the airways narrow and less air can pass through
accept: airways become narrower
extra mucus blocks the airways
airway muscles tighten
misconceptions: the airways get wider
the alveoli burst
mucus stops being produced

question: A pupil writes that an inhaler cures asthma. Why is this incorrect?
answer: There is no cure for asthma; an inhaler only reduces swelling and widens the airways to manage symptoms
options: There is no cure for asthma; an inhaler only reduces swelling and widens the airways to manage symptoms
An inhaler cures asthma but only for a few hours
An inhaler narrows the airways so cannot cure asthma
Only medicine taken as tablets can cure asthma
distractors: an inhaler manages symptoms, it does not cure for any length of time
an inhaler widens the airways, it does not narrow them
there is no cure for asthma in any form, including tablets
misconceptions: asthma can be cured with an inhaler
an inhaler permanently removes asthma

question: Roisin is having an asthma attack and her breathing rate is higher than normal. Explain why.
answer: Her airways are narrower so less oxygen passes through to her lungs
so she breathes faster (pants) to make up for the reduced oxygen
accept: narrower airways let less air in
she breathes faster to get enough oxygen
misconceptions: she breathes faster because the inhaler speeds her up
her airways get wider during an attack
she needs less oxygen during an attack

question: Pollen is a common trigger of asthma. Suggest what effect breathing in pollen could have on someone with asthma.
answer: It could trigger an asthma attack, making the airways narrow so it is harder to breathe
accept: it brings on an asthma attack
it irritates and inflames the airways
misconceptions: pollen cures the asthma
pollen widens the airways
pollen has no effect on asthma

question: Explain how smoking damages the cilia and why this leads to a 'smoker's cough'.
answer: Smoking damages the cilia so they cannot sweep mucus up and out of the trachea efficiently
mucus builds up and the body coughs persistently to try to remove the harmful substances
accept: cilia can no longer clear mucus
mucus collects so the person coughs to clear it
misconceptions: smoking makes the cilia sweep faster
the cough is caused by the lungs filling with air
cilia trap germs directly without mucus

question: Explain how smoking reduces the surface area of the alveoli and how this affects gas exchange.
answer: The hot smoke breaks down the highly folded membranes of the alveoli, forming larger air spaces
this reduces the surface area so less gas exchange can take place
accept: alveoli walls break down so there is less surface
smaller surface area means slower/less diffusion
misconceptions: smoking increases the surface area of alveoli
smoking blocks the alveoli with tar only
reduced surface area speeds up gas exchange

question: Which substance in cigarette smoke is a carcinogen that can lead to mouth, throat or lung cancer?
answer: Tar
distractors: nicotine is addictive and raises heart rate but is not the named carcinogen
carbon monoxide takes the place of oxygen in red blood cells, it is not the carcinogen
oxygen is a normal gas in air and is not harmful in this way
misconceptions: nicotine causes the cancer
carbon monoxide is the carcinogen

question: Explain how carbon monoxide in cigarette smoke affects the body.
answer: Carbon monoxide takes the place of oxygen in the red blood cells
so less oxygen can be carried around the body (which can lead to heart disease)
accept: it replaces oxygen in red blood cells
blood carries less oxygen
misconceptions: carbon monoxide is addictive
carbon monoxide damages the cilia
carbon monoxide adds extra oxygen to the blood

question: Nicotine does not damage the lungs directly. Explain why it is still harmful.
answer: Nicotine is addictive, so it makes the smoker want more cigarettes
it increases heart rate and blood pressure and narrows blood vessels, which can lead to heart disease
accept: it is addictive so people keep smoking
it raises blood pressure and can cause heart disease
misconceptions: nicotine causes lung cancer directly
nicotine is the same as tar
nicotine has no harmful effect at all

question: A person's resting breathing rate is 14 breaths per minute. During exercise it rises to 38 breaths per minute. How many more breaths per minute do they take during exercise?
answer: 24 breaths per minute
misconceptions: adding the two values to get 52
dividing 38 by 14
leaving the answer as 38

question: What is meant by 'gas exchange'?
answer: oxygen moving into the blood and carbon dioxide moving out
accept: oxygen diffuses into the blood and carbon dioxide diffuses out
exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide in the lungs
misconceptions: only oxygen moving in
only carbon dioxide moving out
air moving in and out of the lungs

question: In which part of the lungs does gas exchange take place?
answer: the alveoli
accept: alveolus
air sacs
misconceptions: the bronchi
the trachea
the bronchioles

question: Define diffusion.
answer: the movement of particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration
accept: movement of substances from high to low concentration
spreading from high concentration to low concentration
misconceptions: movement from low to high concentration
particles being pumped across
only happens in liquids

question: By which process do oxygen and carbon dioxide move during gas exchange?
answer: Diffusion
distractors: Breathing moves air in and out but is not how gases cross into the blood
Respiration is the use of oxygen in cells, not the movement of gases in the lungs
Ventilation is air moving in and out of the lungs, not gases crossing the alveolar wall
misconceptions: pupils confuse the gas movement with breathing
pupils confuse gas exchange with respiration

question: In the alveoli, in which direction does oxygen move?
answer: from the alveolus into the blood/capillary
accept: oxygen diffuses from the alveolus into the red blood cells
from the air sac into the bloodstream
misconceptions: oxygen moves from the blood into the alveolus
oxygen moves from capillary to alveolus

question: In the alveoli, in which direction does carbon dioxide move?
answer: from the blood/capillary into the alveolus
accept: carbon dioxide diffuses from the capillary into the alveolus
from the bloodstream into the air sac
misconceptions: carbon dioxide moves from the alveolus into the blood
carbon dioxide moves from alveolus to capillary

question: Explain why oxygen diffuses from the alveolus into the blood.
answer: there is a higher concentration of oxygen in the alveolus than in the capillary
so oxygen diffuses down the concentration gradient from high to low concentration
accept: more oxygen in the alveolus than the blood
oxygen moves from high to low concentration
misconceptions: oxygen is pumped into the blood
oxygen moves from low to high concentration
the blood pulls oxygen in actively

question: Explain why carbon dioxide diffuses from the blood into the alveolus.
answer: there is a higher concentration of carbon dioxide in the capillary than in the alveolus
so carbon dioxide diffuses down the concentration gradient from high to low concentration
accept: more carbon dioxide in the blood than the alveolus
carbon dioxide moves from high to low concentration
misconceptions: carbon dioxide is pushed out actively
carbon dioxide moves from low to high concentration

question: State four ways the alveoli are adapted for efficient gas exchange.
answer: a large surface area
walls that are one cell thick
a good blood supply
a moist surface
accept: thin walls / short diffusion pathway
network of capillaries
moist lining
misconceptions: thick walls for protection
a small surface area
a dry surface

question: Explain how the one-cell-thick walls of the alveoli help gas exchange.
answer: the walls are only one cell thick so there is a short diffusion pathway
gases have a shorter distance to travel so they diffuse across faster
accept: thin walls mean a short diffusion distance
gases only pass through one layer of cells
misconceptions: thin walls let more air be stored
thin walls increase surface area
thicker walls would be faster

question: Explain how having a large surface area increases the rate of gas exchange in the alveoli.
answer: a large surface area means more surfaces are exposed to the gas molecules
so more molecules can diffuse across, increasing the rate of diffusion
accept: bigger area for diffusion
more space for gases to diffuse across
misconceptions: more surface area stores more oxygen
large surface area slows diffusion
surface area has no effect

question: How does the moist surface of the alveoli help gas exchange?
answer: dissolved gases diffuse faster across the membranes, increasing the rate of diffusion
accept: a moist surface speeds up diffusion
gases dissolve and diffuse more quickly
misconceptions: moisture traps the gases
a dry surface would be better
the moisture is for warming the air

question: Explain how a good blood supply helps maintain efficient gas exchange in the alveoli.
answer: the network of capillaries keeps blood flowing
this maintains a concentration difference between the capillary and the alveolus so gases keep diffusing
accept: blood flow keeps a concentration gradient
fresh blood keeps oxygen low and carbon dioxide high in the capillary
misconceptions: blood supply warms the air
more blood means more air storage
blood supply has no effect on diffusion

question: Explain why a highly folded alveolar membrane increases the rate of diffusion.
answer: folding increases the surface area
a larger surface area means more molecules can diffuse across at once, increasing the rate
accept: folds give more surface area for diffusion
more exposed surface for gases
misconceptions: folding makes the membrane thicker
folding stores more oxygen
folding slows diffusion

question: Which gas makes up the highest percentage of the air we inhale?
answer: Nitrogen
distractors: Oxygen is only about 21% of inhaled air, less than nitrogen
Carbon dioxide is only a tiny fraction of inhaled air
Water vapour varies but is a small part of inhaled air
misconceptions: pupils think we only breathe in oxygen
pupils think oxygen is the most abundant gas in air

question: Roughly what percentage of inhaled air is oxygen?
answer: about 21%
accept: 21 per cent
around 21%

question: Roughly what percentage of exhaled air is oxygen?
answer: about 16%
accept: 16 per cent
around 16%

question: Inhaled air is about 21% oxygen and exhaled air is about 16% oxygen. What percentage of the inhaled air is used up in each breath?
Roughly what percentage of exhaled air is carbon dioxide?
answer: about 4%
accept: 4 per cent
around 4%

question: Neelam says we take in all the oxygen we inhale. Explain why this is wrong.
answer: only about 5% of inhaled air is used, so exhaled air still contains about 16% oxygen
accept: we breathe some oxygen back out
exhaled air still has oxygen in it (about 16%)
misconceptions: we use up all the oxygen
exhaled air has no oxygen
we only breathe out carbon dioxide

question: What happens to the nitrogen we inhale?
answer: our bodies do not use it, so we breathe it back out (exhaled air is still about 78% nitrogen)
accept: it is breathed back out unchanged
nitrogen is not used so it leaves the lungs when we exhale
misconceptions: nitrogen is used by the body
nitrogen is turned into carbon dioxide
all the nitrogen stays in the lungs

question: Do we exhale more or less water vapour than we inhale?
answer: we exhale more water vapour than we inhale
accept: more water vapour out than in
exhaled air contains more water vapour
misconceptions: less water vapour out
the same amount of water vapour
no water vapour is exhaled

question: An adult is estimated to have around 300 million alveoli. Written as a number, how many is 300 million?
A scientist measures the wall of a gas exchange surface and finds it is several cells thick. Suggest how this would affect gas exchange and why.
answer: gas exchange would be slower / less efficient
because the diffusion pathway is longer, so gases take longer to diffuse across
accept: thicker walls mean a longer diffusion distance
gases have further to travel so diffuse more slowly
misconceptions: thicker walls speed up diffusion
wall thickness has no effect
thicker walls increase surface area
